article_title: PyTorch Lightning and Intercom-client Hit in Supply Chain Attacks to Steal Credentials
core_thesis: This article reports on a sophisticated software supply chain attack where threat actors compromised the popular PyTorch Lightning Python package (versions 2.6.2 and 2.6.3) and the intercom-client npm/Packagist packages to deploy credential-stealing malware. The attack is part of a broader campaign called Mini Shai-Hulud, attributed to the threat actor TeamPCP, which leverages install-time execution, Bun JavaScript runtime, and obfuscated payloads to harvest credentials from developer environments, CI/CD systems, and cloud services, with worm-like propagation capabilities.
detailed_summary: On April 30, 2026, two malicious versions (2.6.2 and 2.6.3) of the PyTorch Lightning Python package were published on PyPI, compromising the open-source framework used by over 31,100 GitHub stars. The malicious package contained a hidden _runtime directory with a downloader and obfuscated JavaScript payload. Upon importing the lightning module, the attack chain automatically executed: a Python script (start.py) downloaded the Bun JavaScript runtime, which then ran an 11MB obfuscated payload (router_runtime.js) designed to steal credentials comprehensively. Harvested GitHub tokens were validated against api.github.com/user and then used to inject worm-like payloads into up to 50 branches per repository the token could write to, using an upsert operation that impersonated Anthropic's Claude Code. Additionally, the malware modified local npm packages with a postinstall hook, increased patch versions, and repacked .tgz tarballs, so that if the developer published the tampered packages, they would spread to downstream users on npm. The maintainers acknowledged the issue and quarantined the package; the root cause was a compromised PyPI publishing channel, not the GitHub source repository. The malicious versions were live for 42 minutes before quarantine. Separately, version 7.0.4 of intercom-client (npm) and version 5.0.2 of intercom/intercom-php (Packagist) were compromised as part of the same Mini Shai-Hulud campaign, using similar techniques: preinstall hooks, Composer plugin execution, and the same router_runtime.js payload. The campaign is attributed to TeamPCP (also calling themselves LAPSUS$), who have launched a dark web onion site. The intercom compromise was traced back to a local install of pyannote-audio, which introduced the malicious Lightning package as a transitive dependency, showing downstream effects from prior TeamPCP waves.

entities.name: PyTorch Lightning
entities.role: Open-source Python framework for PyTorch, whose PyPI package was compromised in a supply chain attack.

entities.name: TeamPCP
entities.role: Threat actor group responsible for the Mini Shai-Hulud campaign, also known as LAPSUS$.

entities.name: Mini Shai-Hulud
entities.role: Broader supply chain attack campaign targeting multiple package ecosystems (npm, PyPI, Packagist) with credential-stealing malware.

entities.name: Socket
entities.role: Security firm that analyzed the malicious packages and provided detailed technical analysis.

entities.name: Aikido Security
entities.role: Security firm that reported the compromise of PyTorch Lightning.

entities.name: OX Security
entities.role: Security firm involved in detecting the attack.

entities.name: StepSecurity
entities.role: Security firm involved in detecting the attack.

entities.name: Intercom
entities.role: Company whose npm and Packagist packages (intercom-client, intercom/intercom-php) were compromised.

entities.name: PyPI
entities.role: Python Package Index repository where malicious Lightning versions were published and later quarantined.

entities.name: Bun
entities.role: JavaScript runtime downloaded and used by the malware to execute the obfuscated payload.

entities.name: router_runtime.js
entities.role: 11MB obfuscated JavaScript payload used for credential theft and propagation.

key_data_points: Two malicious versions (2.6.2 and 2.6.3) of PyTorch Lightning were published on PyPI on April 30, 2026, and were live for 42 minutes before quarantine (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates the speed of supply chain attacks and the need for rapid detection).

The attack chain automatically executes upon importing the lightning module, requiring no user action after installation (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: highlights the danger of install-time execution; can be used to design similar stealthy payloads).

The malware uses Bun JavaScript runtime to run an 11MB obfuscated payload (router_runtime.js) that steals credentials from GitHub, npm, SSH keys, cloud credentials, Kubernetes, Vault, Docker, .env files, and CI/CD secrets (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: shows a comprehensive credential harvesting approach that can be emulated in red team exercises).

Harvested GitHub tokens are validated against api.github.com/user and then used to inject worm-like payloads into up to 50 branches per repository via an upsert operation, impersonating Anthropic's Claude Code (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates a propagation technique that can be used to simulate lateral movement).

The malware also modifies local npm packages with a postinstall hook, increases patch version, and repacks .tgz tarballs, enabling spread to downstream users if the developer publishes the tampered packages (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: illustrates a method for persistent compromise of developer environments).

The intercom-client npm package (version 7.0.4) and intercom/intercom-php Packagist package (version 5.0.2) were compromised using similar techniques, including Composer plugin execution and the same router_runtime.js payload (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: shows cross-ecosystem propagation).

The root cause of the Lightning compromise was a compromised PyPI publishing channel, not the GitHub source repository (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: emphasizes the importance of securing CI/CD pipelines and package publishing credentials).

The intercom compromise was traced to a local install of pyannote-audio, which introduced the malicious Lightning package as a transitive dependency (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: highlights the risk of transitive dependencies in supply chain attacks).
conclusions: The PyTorch Lightning supply chain attack demonstrates that even widely-used, well-maintained open-source projects can be compromised through their package publishing channels, bypassing source code review. The attack's speed (42 minutes live) and automatic execution upon import make it highly dangerous. Users should immediately block versions 2.6.2 and 2.6.3, downgrade to 2.6.1, and rotate any credentials exposed in affected environments.

The Mini Shai-Hulud campaign, attributed to TeamPCP, shows a deliberate and sustained effort to compromise multiple package ecosystems (npm, PyPI, Packagist) using consistent techniques: install-time execution, Bun-based payload delivery, obfuscated JavaScript, credential harvesting, and GitHub abuse for propagation. This indicates a well-resourced threat actor with a focus on developer environments.

The intercom compromise being traced back to a transitive dependency (pyannote-audio) highlights the cascading nature of supply chain attacks: one compromised package can become a bridge into additional ecosystems. This underscores the need for rigorous dependency management and monitoring of transitive dependencies in development workflows.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your Red Teaming and cybersecurity interests as it details a real-world supply chain attack with specific techniques that can be emulated in offensive security exercises: install-time execution, credential harvesting from multiple sources (GitHub, cloud, CI/CD), worm-like propagation via GitHub tokens, and cross-ecosystem spread (npm, PyPI, Packagist). As a programmer, understanding how malicious packages can compromise developer environments is crucial for securing your own pipelines and for designing realistic red team scenarios. The article also provides actionable indicators (malicious versions, payload names, exfiltration domains) that can be used for detection or simulation. Additionally, the mention of transitive dependencies is a key lesson for your homelab and DevOps practices: always audit your dependency trees.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a honeypot environment that mimics a developer workstation with exposed PyPI/npm publishing credentials and monitor for similar attack patterns. This could help you detect supply chain attacks early and practice incident response. Also, implement a CI/CD pipeline that automatically checks for malicious packages using tools like Socket or OSSF Scorecard, and enforce dependency pinning with hash verification.
